---
title: "An Attentional Blink Study"
author: "Trisha Theivendiram, Troy Bauce, & Savera Sethi"
format: revealjs
css: styles.css
---

## Abstract

- Investigated the attentional blink phenomenon  
- Used a rapid serial visual presentation (RSVP) task  
- Measured accuracy of identifying a second target (T2)  
- Compared performance across short vs. long lag conditions  
- Found differences in accuracy depending on lag timing  

---

## Introduction

- Attention is limited despite large amounts of incoming information  
- Attentional blink = brief “blind spot” after processing a stimulus  
- RSVP tasks test how timing affects perception of targets  
- T2 identification depends on when it appears after T1  
- Hypothesis: higher accuracy at Lag 1 vs. Lag 5  

---

## Methods

- Participants completed task individually on a computer  
- Programmed in Python using Pygame  
- Stimuli presented at 100 ms per item (RSVP format)  
- Two conditions: Lag 1 (immediate) vs. Lag 5 (delayed)  
- Accuracy recorded as correct (1) or incorrect (0)  

---

## Results

- Accuracy compared between Lag 1 and Lag 5 conditions  
- Lag 1: higher accuracy (M = 0.71, n = 38)  
- Lag 5: lower accuracy (M = 0.48, n = 46)  
- Significant difference: t(80) = 2.20, p < .05  
- Results support effect of lag on attention  

---

## Discussion

- Findings support hypothesis (Lag 1 > Lag 5 accuracy)  
- Suggests temporal proximity improves target processing  
- Supports attentional blink and processing limitation theories  
- Limitations: small sample, learning effects, slower presentation speed  
- Future improvements: practice trials, faster stimuli, more participants  

---

## Conclusion

- Attentional blink task successfully implemented  
- Lag significantly influenced target detection accuracy  
- Shorter lag improved performance  
- Demonstrates limits of attentional processing  
- Provides a reproducible experimental framework  

---

## References

- Attentional blink (RSVP) task – Free template and step-by-step guide. (n.d.). *Testable*. https://www.testable.org/experiment-guides/attention/attentional-blink-task  

- Dale, G., & Arnell, K. M. (2011). How reliable is the attentional blink? Examining the relationships within and between attentional blink tasks over time. *Psychological Research, 77*(2), 99–105. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00426-011-0403-y  

- Martens, S., & Wyble, B. (2010). The attentional blink: Past, present, and future of a blind spot in perceptual awareness. *Neuroscience & Biobehavioral Reviews, 34*(6), 947–957. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.neubiorev.2009.12.005  

- Visser, T. A., & Ohan, J. L. (2012). How does information processing speed relate to the attentional blink? *PLoS ONE, 7*(3). https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0033265
